canonical_definition: In the provided literature, narrative understanding is defined as a latent ability to capture and interpret the key details and implications of a story. This construct is operationalized through the behavior of summarization, which is explicitly framed as a means of assessment. As one study notes, "summarization is a vehicle for examining the understanding the summarizer has of the story" ("Comparing Specialised Small and General Large Language Models on Text Classification: 100 Labelled Samples to Achieve Break-Even Performance"). This approach suggests that the act of producing or evaluating a summary, which involves "capturing and distilling the key details and takeways from that narrative," serves as the observable evidence for this underlying ability. Additionally, the construct is noted in one paper as being related to language proficiency.
papers: Comparing Specialised Small and General Large Language Models on Text Classification: 100 Labelled Samples to Achieve Break-Even Performance
LiTEx: A Linguistic Taxonomy of Explanations for Understanding Within-Label Variation in Natural Language Inference
Power doesn’t reside in size: A Low Parameter Hybrid Language Model (HLM) for Sentiment Analysis in Code-mixed data

definitions.text: The latent ability to capture and interpret the key details and implications of a story when producing or evaluating a summary.
definitions.paper: Comparing Specialised Small and General Large Language Models on Text Classification: 100 Labelled Samples to Achieve Break-Even Performance

relationships.paper: DRPruning: Efficient Large Language Model Pruning through Distributionally Robust Optimization

acl25: DRPruning: Efficient Large Language Model Pruning through Distributionally Robust Optimization
